sanskrit_devanagari:
कर्मणो ह्यपि बोद्धव्यं बोद्धव्यं च विकर्मणः ।
अकर्मणश्च बोद्धव्यं गहना कर्मणो गतिः ॥ १७ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
karmaṇo hy api boddhavyaṁ boddhavyaṁ ca vikarmaṇaḥ |
akarmaṇaś ca boddhavyaṁ gahanā karmaṇo gatiḥ || 17 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: The intricacies of action are very hard to understand. Therefore one should know properly what action is, what forbidden action is and what inaction is.
The intricacies of action are very hard to understand. Therefore one should know properly what action is, what forbidden action is, and what inaction is.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 973 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: If one is serious about liberation from material bondage, one has to understand the distinctions between action, inaction and unauthorized actions. One has to apply oneself to such an analysis of action, reaction and perverted actions because it is a very difficult subject matter.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 4.17 Hi, for; there is something boddhavyam, to be known; api, even; karmanah, about action enjoined by the scriptures; and there is certainly something to be known vikarmanah, about prohibited action; so, also, there is something to be known akarmanah, about inaction, about sitting quietly.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-4-17-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads vikarma as 'prohibited action' (action contrary to dharma) — the third term that complicates the simple action/inaction dyad. The threefold typology (right action, prohibited action, inaction) must all be discriminated. Gahanā gatiḥ signals: this is genuinely hard, not merely rhetorical difficulty.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 4.17 There is something which ought to be known in regard to action (Karma) which forms the means of attaining release. So also is the case in regard to ‘multi-form or varied forms of action’ (Vikarma).
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-4-17-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the threefold typology as karma (Vedic-prescribed action), vikarma (action against the Veda), and akarma (the abstention from action which itself is a karma in a sense). All three require understanding. The way of action is deep because what looks like inaction may be karma, what looks like karma may be akarma — the surface does not disclose the doctrinal status.

positions.rendering: prohibited action / action contrary to dharma

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three read vikarma as 'wrong-action' — forbidden, prohibited, or against the Vedic order. The verse is uncontroversial on this point. The interesting term is akarma — what counts as 'inaction.' The classical commentators split on whether akarma is mere abstention or whether it is a positive non-doership state. 4.18 will resolve this.

literal_meaning: The truth of action is to be known, and the truth of forbidden action, and the truth of inaction; the way of action is profound.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna names a threefold typology: karma (right action), vikarma (forbidden action / wrong action), and akarma (inaction). All three must be understood (boddhavyam). The closing claim — gahanā karmaṇo gatiḥ — names the depth: the way of action is profound, deep, hard to penetrate. The verse is the philosophical setup for the headline 4.18.
